data.title: Dynagas LNG: A Speculative Long Position Could Be Justified
data.description:
Introduction

Dynagas LNG Partners (DLNG) is a shipping company (technically it's an LP, but I will use the word 'company' to refer to Dynagas) operating a fleet of six LNG carriers. All carriers are currently employed with a weighted average charter term of 5.2 years with a contractual backlog in excess of $800M, but three of the vessels will have to be redeployed in 2028. The strong cash flows have allowed the company to rapidly reduce its net debt, while one series of preferred stock was retired last summer.

Robust Results, and This Will Continue for the Next Two Years

While we still have to wait for the company to report on its final quarter of 2025, considering the vessels have been chartered out at known rates, I don’t expect any major changes.

In the third quarter of 2025, the total revenue remained virtually unchanged at almost $39M. Meanwhile, the operating expenses increased slightly, mainly due to higher voyage expenses, partially reduced by slightly lower vessel operating expenses. This resulted in an operating income of approximately $18.8M, which is approximately $1M lower than in the third quarter of 2024.

This didn’t have a major impact on the bottom-line result, as the net interest expenses continued to decrease. That being said, the 50% increase in net income, which jumped to $18.7M, or $0.48 per share, was mainly caused by an increase in the ‘other income,’ which jumped to $5.2M. This was related to the income from insurance claims for damages incurred in prior years and should be seen as a non-recurring item.

As the company barely has any capital expenditures (the capex bill tends to be zero in most quarters), the net free cash flow, of course, exceeds the net income. As you can see below, the reported operating cash flow was $26.5M (including a net cash investment in the working capital position), while no capital expenditures were incurred.

This also means the net free cash flow was $26.5M, which is in excess of 40% higher than the reported net income, due to the difference between depreciation and amortization expenses and the actual incurred capex.

Note: this does not include the preferred dividend payments yet. The sole remaining series that’s still outstanding is the fixed-rate 9% preferred stock (which I will discuss later in this article). As there are 3M preferred shares outstanding, the quarterly preferred dividends are just $1.7M. So even after isolating the non-recurring ‘other income’ from the equation, it goes without saying the preferred dividends are still very well covered. In fact, as Dynagas retired its Series B preferred stock, the payout ratio is now even lower than before.

And despite retiring the preferred stock, the balance sheet remains very healthy, both from the perspective of the net debt level, as well as the total amount of equity. Although Dynagas does pay a distribution on its common shares, the majority of its income is retained on the balance sheet. And despite retiring $55M in preferred stock, the total amount of equity on the balance sheet decreased by just $23M in the first nine months of 2025.

With almost $35M in cash on the balance sheet, the liquidity remains strong, and Dynagas recently increased its distribution to $0.05 per quarter per share. This was predicted by fellow author Nikolaos Sismanis in his September article,

The one caveat going forward is that I would like to see contract extensions as soon as possible.

While the average remaining charter term is 5.2 years (with $850M in confirmed revenue), there are three vessels that will need new employment from 2028 on. While that’s of course still two years away, it will be a key element in determining what to do with the position. And also keep in mind two vessels are employed under charter agreements transporting LNG from the Russia-based Yamal LNG project to the buyers. Dynagas currently isn’t subject to Russia-related sanctions, but it also is an important factor to keep an eye on.

After Retiring the Series B Preferred Stock, Only the Series A Remains Outstanding

In the summer of 2025, Dynagas retired the Series B floating rate preferred stock, which means the Series A (DLNG.PR.A) is the sole outstanding series of preferred capital. This preferred share offers a fixed 9% preferred dividend ($2.25 per share, payable in four equal quarterly tranches of $0.5625 per share).

As the preferred shares are trading at a premium of approximately 5.5% to the $25 call price, the current yield is just under 8.6%. Given the low payout ratio versus the net income and the free cash flow, the preferred shares offer an excellent dividend coverage ratio, but keep in mind this series of preferred shares can be retired at any given moment. I don’t think this is a near-term risk, as creditors likely wouldn’t be too keen on the company spending $75M on yet another preferred stock buyback, but the risk cannot be ruled out, as DLNG also just announced a share repurchase program on its common stock. So while I don’t think these shares will be called in the very near future, it is a risk investors should be aware of.

Investment Thesis

At the end of the third quarter, the book value per common share exceeded $10, so while the stock isn’t very interesting from an income perspective, the good earnings visibility for the next two years should bode well for the share price. Of course it will be very interesting to see what will happen once existing charters have to be renewed, as we have recently seen competitors deciding to scrap older vessels.

If income is important, then the preferred shares could still be interesting. Although they are callable at any given moment, the distribution hike and recently announced common share buyback may indicate Dynagas is in no rush to retire its 9% yielding preferred stock.

I have a small position in both the common stock as well as the preferred stock. I would consider selling the common shares closer to $6-7 (60-70% of the book value, unless Dynagas provides more clarity on potential charters from 2028 on). At the current share price of less than $3.70, the stock is trading at just 1/3 of the book value, likely due to the uncertainty related to the fair value of older vessels, as well as the charter maturities in 2028 for three of the vessels. That’s why—despite the strong earnings and cash flows—I still consider DLNG a speculative long position. I feel more at ease with the preferred stock, but that represents a small position in my portfolio as well.

data.title: Carnival's Diversified Destinations Deliver Resilient Booking Trends - Rally Still Has Legs
data.description:
I previously covered Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL) in November 2025, discussing how the cruise company had reported the durable consumer demand for cruising, as observed in the raised FY2025 guidance and the management achieving the 2026 SEA Change targets 18 months ahead of schedule.

Combined with the excellent 2026 booking trends at higher prices, the growing customer deposits, the expanded investments in its new fleet through 2033/private islands through 2026, and the tailwinds from the new loyalty program, I had reiterated my Buy rating upon a moderate retracement then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the CCL stock, thanks to its much needed return to "investment grade leverage metrics" and the consequently reinstated dividend payouts, significantly aided by the promising FY2026 guidance and the healthier balance sheet.

This is on top of the reduced exposure to the Caribbean oversupply risks, balanced by the robust booking trends in the EU, as observed in the elevated customer deposits.

CCL Hints At FY2026/FY2027 Outperformance

CCL 1Y Stock Price

Since then, my dip Buy recommendation had materialized during the worst of the November 2025 correction at the $24s, between the established intermediate support line at the $26s and the base support line at the $20s.

This was before the bulls defended CCL's recovery, significantly aided by the robust performance metrics observed in the FQ4'25 earnings call on December 19, 2025 along with the reinstated dividend after the last announced payout during the early days of COVID-19 pandemic in January 2020.

While the initial quarterly dividend of $0.15 per share payable on February 27, 2026 is relatively underwhelming compared to the last payout of $0.50 per share, I am not overly concerned indeed, since it already signals their return to "investment grade leverage metrics."

This is on top of the management's confidence about their "ability to generate sustainable cash flow, disciplined capital allocation strategy and strong financial position," while returning great value to long-term share holders, partly aided by the lack of new fleet delivery in 2026.

While CCL's return to investment grade is somewhat delayed compared to its direct competitor, Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) in July 2024, I am of the opinion that it is better to be late than never.

This is especially since CCL now boasts a relatively richer forward dividend yield of ~1.8%, based on the current stock prices of $31.69 and the annualized dividend payout of $0.60 per share, compared to RCL at ~1.32%.

CCL appears more than well capitalized to maintain a secure income investment thesis, based on the FY2025 Free Cash Flow generation at $2.6B (+100% YoY), against the estimated annualized dividend obligation of $0.84B (based on the FQ4'25 shares outstanding at 1.4B and the $0.60 annualized payout per share).

Moving forward, CCL has already offered a promising FY2026 guidance, with a net yield growth of +2.5% YoY in constant currency (compared to FY2025 levels of +5.5%), adj EBITDA of $7.63B (+5.9% YoY), and adj EPS of $2.48 (+10.2% YoY).

When compared to the planned capacity growth by +0.9% YoY in FY2026, it is apparent that the management has priced in higher ticket prices and onboard spending per diem trends ahead.

With part of its FY2026/FY2027 capacity already booked at "historical high prices for both North America and Europe," aided by the insights offered by the higher customer deposits at $7.24B (+1.4% QoQ/+6.9% YoY), CCL appears well positioned to weather the industry wide headwinds in 2026.

For one, CCL (similar to RCL and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH)) already announces an enhanced loyalty program, with it expected to trigger a slight bottom-line impact on its net yield growth through FY2027 albeit with the added benefit of "improving customer engagement and increasing customer lifetime value."

Secondly, readers must note that NCLH has doubled down on their family friendly pricing, their expanded Caribbean capacity to over 50%, and the shorter sailings/excursions - all of which are expected to trigger sales headwinds to RCL, since "the Caribbean represents 57% of our deployment this year and 63% of capacity in the fourth quarter."

Fortunately for readers, CCL is less exposed to the Caribbean cruising oversupply risks, since the region only comprise 35% of its cruising capacity in 2026, aided by the robust booking trends in the EU, with the EU comprising 31% of the company's capacity.

As a result of the promising FY2026 guidance and the robust booking trends, I can understand why the CCL stock has been able to enjoy an outsized rally of +27.9% from the recent November 2025 bottom of $24s.

This is significantly aided by the healthier net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 3.4x in FQ4'25, down from 4.3x in FQ4'24 and the peak levels of -18.1x in FQ4'22 (author estimated), while notably nearer to the 2.02x observed in FY2019 (author estimated).

This is especially since CCL appears well capitalized to deliver both shareholder return across dividend payouts, opportunistic share repurchases (18M shares retired as of FQ4'25 earnings call), and balance sheet deleveraging to the target ratio of under 3x ahead, thanks to the richer cash flows.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Based on CCL's thrice raised FY2025 guidance after the consecutive double beat performance over the past twelve quarters (aside from the one-time FQ4'25 top-line miss by -0.6%), it seems that the consensus is pricing in another beat and raise performance based on the slightly higher FY2026 adj EPS estimates of $2.54 (+12.8% YoY) against the management's current guidance of $2.48 (+10.2% YoY).

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that the current consensus forward estimates appear achievable, with CCL expected to report an excellent top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +4.2%/+12.3% through FY2028.

CCL Valuations

Thanks to the ongoing stock price volatility over the past few months, CCL remains compellingly valued at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 8.84x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 12.63x as well, compared to the 1Y mean of 8.59x/12.62x and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 9.63x/14.50x, respectively.

Based on the projected recovery in its adj EPS at a 3Y CAGR of +12.3%, CCL appears reasonable at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.02x as well, compared to the 5Y pre-pandemic ratio of 0.78x, the sector median of 1.80x, and the direct cruise peers, including:

  • RCL at 0.96x (using the same methodology),
  • Viking Holdings (VIK) at 1.00x,
  • NCLH at 0.65x, and
  • Disney (DIS) at 1.51x,

with it apparent that the CCL continues to offer a compellingly valued recovery story despite the +110.2% rally from the April 2025 bottom and the +297.6% rally from the April 2020 bottom.

CCL Remains A Great Buy Upon A Dip

CCL 5Y Stock Price

For now, CCL continues to trade well within the established trading pattern since the October 2022 bottom, with it providing interested investors with a great insight into its future stock price movement.

Based on the 5Y pre-pandemic P/E mean of 14.50x (cheap compared to the peer group mean of ~17.98x) and the FY2025 adj EPS performance of $2.25 (+58.4% YoY), it is apparent that CCL is trading near to my fair value estimates of $32.60.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $3.18, there is an excellent upside potential of +45.4% to my long-term price target of $46.10 as well, despite the recent bounce off the 200 day moving averages/support levels of $24s.

Combined with the reinstatement of the dividend payouts and the healthier balance sheet, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the CCL stock.

Caveats Linked To Buy Rating

For now, interested investors may want to observe the stock price movement for a little longer, since it is uncertain if CCL may be able to break out of the 2021/2025 resistance levels of $31s.

With the FQ4'25 earnings call behind us and two more months to go to its upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call sometime in March 2026, I am of the opinion that, pending further near-term catalysts, the stock is likely to lose part of its recent gains and consolidate nearer to the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

Combined with the potential selling pressure after the ex-dividend date of February 27, 2026, my reiterated Buy rating for the CCL stock comes with the caveat that interested investors wait for a dip buying opportunity for a lower dollar cost average and potentially, the expanded dividend yields of >2%.

data.title: AI Reset Is Complete; Tech's Next Leg Starts Here
data.description:
The market rotation is in full swing, but tech stocks aren't collapsing beyond what the current reallocation themes have already inflicted on these previous outperformers. Tech (XLK) as a whole has kind of struggled (relatively speaking) as the sector continues to face such rotation pressure, as the Dow Jones (DJI) (DJX) and S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) less the Magnificent Seven continued on their gallant advance. Yet, to think that since tech comprises 35% of the S&P 500 and that the Magnificent Seven comprises >31% of the SPX's market valuation, can such a disconnect persist through early 2026 without the "generals" starting to regain their mojo and exert their clout again in driving the market's advance? In my opinion, highly unlikely.

The fears of the AI bubble haven't gone away, and in all honesty, that's also a good thing, isn't it? We want people to stay cautious amidst the AI exuberance, indicating that the market hasn't quite gone into the extreme FOMO phase yet (that usually precedes a bull trap toppish pattern), and there's also no letup at all when we consider how early we are still in the multi-trillion AI infrastructure buildout phase, with enterprises still nascent in their AI adoption phase (but gaining cadence with more moving into production). I'm sure many of us are keenly scrutinizing when this massive adoption wave could be occurring, so that investors can start baking in the monetization thesis that could be used to justify the unprecedented AI spending fervor that is happening right now, and not stopping anytime soon yet.

Despite the pullback, there is little in the way that suggests the AI trade is going to roll over anytime soon. Meta Platforms securing its nuclear power deals (META) to lower the risks of allowing power to become a key chokepoint in stymying the execution themes. Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) just unveiled a robust December sales performance that shows just how resilient this upcycle has proved to be. How about OpenAI (OPENAI), which is reportedly raising more funds at a markedly higher valuation of $750B, despite facing onerous headwinds from Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) catching up? Or how about Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang (NVDA) just unveiled this week at CES that Vera Rubin is primed to go into volume production from the second half of 2026?

Hence, if there's really a deep slowdown that could throttle the market's confidence, it's just not showing in the market tape (price action). As seen above, the XLK remains delicately perched with higher-lows and higher-highs price action indicating solidly resilient momentum swings that favor an advancing cadence, and not something sinister underneath that could precede a deeper and more destructive reckoning that could send bulls sprawling for cover quickly.

In addition, even the insider buy/sell ratios are demonstrating that insiders are using the current pessimism (or underperformance) to ramp up their allocations to tech, and not fleeing. Do these insiders think that the AI bubble is about to implode from their actions? Surely not, right?

Therefore, if anything, I think the rotation themes will be short-term in nature, and not expected to be structurally "destructive" on the main AI growth and tech themes when these stocks pick up steam later.

Furthermore, how often do we get the sector more than nearly 10% undervalued, as assessed with Morningstar's sector fair value estimates? It's a notable departure from the past three years, when we consider the 2021/22 bear market decline and last year's Liberation Day capitulation that saw tech stocks falling into more significantly undervalued zones. In other words, this really does seem like an opportunity for us to get more involved, and not less.

Next, how often do we get NVDA printing a forward earnings multiple of just >26x, which isn't far from the tech median of 25.6x, and the AI King is expected to deliver free cash flow of about >$200B by FY2028, with margins approaching the 50% mark.

Nevertheless, I think the market is also signaling that they want to shift out of the Mag-7 thesis or the AI trade to less expensive sectors in the market. However, if being relatively undervalued is all that matters, then the setup will be easy peasy, right? All of us just need to press the "Sell" button on our core tech/growth holdings and flip it immediately and "nonchalantly" to these undervalued sectors and buy aggressively right now.

And if that's the case, I believe we will not see the tech-heavy Nasdaq insiders (NDX) (QQQ) taking advantage of the current consolidation to load up during this pullback, and we will also certainly see the growth stocks moving into a downtrend (but that's not what I've observed thus far).

Plus, Wall Street still expects the Mag-7 to continue printing highly resilient and visible medium-term earnings growth outlooks (>20%) for the next three to five years. Plus, I believe if there is a group of companies that possesses the massive wherewithal, the businesses, and the structural and proven ability to monetize AI technologies (while still minting robust FCF in their core businesses), I think it's hard to fathom that our best tech companies aren't right there getting themselves ready to capitalize and charge ahead as AI proliferates deeper into the enterprise, with more companies moving from the experimentation to production phase.

Now, obviously, I'm not going to paint all nice and rosy out there. President Trump's so-called "Donroe Doctrine" is causing foreign policy watchers much consternation, as they reconsider the long term ramifications for the US and its global peers, especially regarding the reaction from China. The short-term reaction has been relatively muted, as the Venezuelan situation doesn't seem to be boiling over or worsening right now. Yet, we must be prepared that heightened geopolitical tensions could affect the market's confidence, as the US becomes a less predictable anchor and "guardian" of well-established international norms, while also affording leverage to China and Russia to exert more clout and pressure in their regional spheres of influence.

These worries haven't started to pan out yet, but they are worth paying heed to. In this case, I think the bullish narrative on the defense trade should persist, especially since President Trump wants to lift the 2027 defense budget to $1.5T. How it will be adequately financed and whether it's achievable and the follow-on effects will be keenly discussed and deliberated in the coming months, but the market sentiments have already bolstered related defense plays. These developments could also take the wind and mojo from the dominant AI narrative, as geopolitical factors and influences become an increasingly pivotal setup, with the market plausibly marking down the AI premium that has been a signature of the tech-driven advance so far.

Nevertheless, the US unemployment rate has finally started to show signs of an abatement, having consistently crept up from the 2023 lows, dropping to 4.4% as of the most recent reading. Consumer confidence has also risen from a long-term low, suggesting that 2026 might not be as somber as some long-term bears could have envisioned.

While a January interest rate reduction is likely out of the question as of now with the economy staying incredibly resilient, the market is still pricing in two cuts in the next 12 months, corroborating a largely dovish Fed posture. Also, inflation rates are still expected to stay relatively in control, validating the strength of the current position, and mitigating unanticipated reversals from the Fed into hawkish signaling.

Therefore, I believe that as long as the specter of a recession isn't what the market is expected to price in, I believe the heightened multiples right now of >22x forward earnings can continue to be sustained, with overall earnings growth continuing on an upward trend through the rest of the year, and possibly longer.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kura Sushi USA Still Appears Expensive Here
data.description:
My thesis is that Kura Sushi may be a buy right now because of its price action. Unlike most of the other stocks I’m following, for several years Kura Sushi USA’s stock price has displayed a pattern of peaks and troughs. I included a chart of the company’s stock price in the first section of this article to illustrate this pattern. Most recently, I gave this company a hold rating when its stock price was at the bottom of a trough on the chart, and now it may be rising toward a peak again. There are fundamental reasons for why Kura Sushi USA could post better financial results in 2026 as well, so my rating is not just based on the chart. But this stock is also trading at a high valuation right now, so its strengths may be priced in.

In its first-quarter earnings report, Kura Sushi USA reported that its revenue rose 13.5% to $73.5 million even though it also posted -2.5% comps and a $3.1 million loss for the quarter. Kura Sushi USA also said that its restaurant-level operating margin fell to 15.1% in the quarter. But this company’s stock price rose 16.7% on January 8 anyway, and there are two reasons for that. Kura Sushi USA posted better-than-expected top-line results, and it reaffirmed its full-year guidance.

Kura Sushi USA still believes that it can achieve a full-year restaurant-level operating margin of 18%, and it’s still planning to add 16 new restaurants this year. And it raised its menu prices in November as well. Additionally, it has quarters with easy comps coming up and will benefit from upcoming collaborations in those quarters. The company’s business model hasn’t changed either, and it’s still reducing its operating costs by automating its restaurants. In the long term, Kura Sushi USA will have higher operating margins and lower labor costs than other restaurants because of its robots.

Technical Analysis Is More Relevant for Kura Sushi USA

As I’ve explained in my previous articles, Kura Sushi USA’s stock price has been fluctuating over the past few years. I’m not sure why its price follows this unusual pattern, because other restaurants’ stock prices don’t behave like this. But if you look at the chart, this stock’s price could rise back up to the $90-$100 range in the next few quarters. This might be a better stock to trade rather than hold for a long-term investment, though, because after Kura Sushi USA’s price peaks, it might fall quickly again.

[object HTMLElement]

Kura Sushi USA Has Easy Comps Coming Up

Kura Sushi USA had a tougher year than usual in 2025. After reporting 1.8% comps in 1Q 2025, it posted -5.3% comps in 2Q 2025. On the call, management blamed the LA fires for the restaurant’s weak results, and also pointed out that the restaurant benefited from a Peanuts collaboration in 2024 that it didn’t have in 2025. Now, in 2Q 2026, the restaurant is benefiting from its current Kirby collaboration, and management’s remarks on the call indicate that the Kirby collaboration is going well.

Kura Sushi USA also posted -2.1% comps in 3Q 2025, so it has an easy comparison coming up in 3Q 2026 as well. Again, this restaurant doesn’t always post consistent results because some of its collaborations are more effective than others. Kura Sushi USA has provided information about its upcoming collaborations. On the call, SVP Benjamin Porten said, “So Kirby runs through the end of January, and then we have Sanrio for February. And then March and April, we have Jujutsu Kaisen to coincide with our new anime season.”

In my previous article, I also explained that Kura Sushi USA’s guidance for 2026 does not include the uplift from collaborations because management doesn’t want to rely on collaborations to meet investors’ expectations. It’s not the only company with management that feels this way. Bath & Body Works (BBWI) disappointed investors last quarter when its Disney (DIS) Villains collaboration didn’t go as well as expected. But I also think that because of this factor, Kura Sushi USA could outperform its guidance and investors’ expectations in 2Q and 3Q 2026.

Kura Sushi USA Is Using Automation to Make Itself More Competitive

Kura Sushi USA has been using automated conveyor belts and server robots to reduce its operating costs, so it’s already a high-tech restaurant. Now, the sushi bar is installing automated dishwashers to clean off its sushi plates, but it sounds like it’ll take more time to fully roll out these dishwashers. On the 1Q 2026 call, Porten explained that the dishwashers aren’t likely to have a large impact on Kura Sushi USA’s 2026 results, but more of them will be installed in 2027.

Automation could create additional expansion opportunities for Kura Sushi USA as well, although the reason for this is somewhat sad. This is a large, publicly traded company that can buy and install robots to increase its efficiency. Family-owned Japanese restaurants can’t afford to do this, so they will have to raise their menu prices by larger amounts in 2026 because of rising labor costs. And these restaurants will also be paying for high tariffs on imported food. If consumers can’t afford the independent restaurants’ price increases, some of these restaurants may shut down in 2026.

Tariffs on Imported Toys Are A Risk Factor

Because Kura Sushi USA relies on its collaborations to drive traffic, it’s exposed to a risk factor that other restaurants don’t need to worry about. This sushi bar imports toys from China and gives them away to its customers, which means that it has to pay high tariffs on these toys. As the CFO explained on the call, “Most of our promotional materials come from China. So our Bikkura Pon toys or giveaway items, those come from China, and they've been experiencing pretty heavy tariffs.”

While retail stores and manufacturers have been importing inventory from Southeast Asian countries where tariffs are lower to get around high tariffs on Chinese products, it might be harder for Kura Sushi USA to do that. This sushi bar is giving away unique collectibles, not commodity products, and it could take time to find other suppliers. Going by management’s remarks on the call, it sounded like Kura Sushi USA was able to negotiate with suppliers to reduce its food costs, but might not have been able to do that with its toy suppliers. So, Kura Sushi USA will remain vulnerable to additional tariffs on Chinese imports in 2026.

Updated Comparative Valuation for Kura Sushi USA

Kura Sushi USA is rapidly adding new restaurants, so I’d compare it to other restaurants that are rapidly expanding. This is a growth stock. After the market close on January 9, Kura Sushi USA had a forward EV/sales of 2.95 and a forward P/S of 2.54. Meanwhile, Dutch Bros (BROS) had a forward EV/sales of 5.50 and a forward P/S of 4.90. And CAVA Group (CAVA) had a forward EV/sales of 7.06 and a forward P/S of 7.01. So, Kura Sushi USA is trading at a discount to peers on revenue metrics.

But Kura Sushi USA is also trading at a forward EV/EBITDA of 45. That’s higher than Dutch Bros’ forward EV/EBITDA of 31 and lower than CAVA’s EV/EBITDA of 55. And I previously said I thought CAVA was overpriced. So, if I use EV/EBITDA like I did last time, Kura Sushi USA doesn’t appear to be trading at a discount to other restaurant growth stocks.

Updated Kura Sushi USA Valuation

Kura Sushi USA maintained its guidance for an 18% full-year restaurant-level operating margin and 20% location growth in 2026. I think this margin is achievable because the sushi bar raised its menu prices in November 2025. In my previous article, I also calculated that Kura Sushi USA could achieve a 6.4% EBITDA margin if this occurred. I also calculated that, including an uplift from 20% location growth, this restaurant could generate 24% revenue growth in 2026.

On the call, Kura Sushi USA’s management also called for $330 to $334 million in full-year revenue. At the midpoint of $332 million, that’s 17% revenue growth. I think Kura Sushi USA’s revenue growth guidance for 2026 is too low. It sounds like management still plans to add 16 new locations and also expects positive comps. So, I think my previous forecast for 24% revenue growth in 2026 is more realistic.

So, I’ll maintain my forecast for $350.7 million in revenue and $22.4 million in EBITDA with a 6.4% EBITDA margin. Kura Sushi USA’s EV/EBITDA is 45 right now, which is high compared to other restaurants, so its EV/EBITDA is likely to decrease. If I project that its EV/EBITDA will fall to 40 by the end of 2026, it would have an EV of $896 million. After subtracting $170 million in debt and adding $62 million in cash, its market cap would be $792 million. I’d also maintain my projection that this sushi bar’s diluted share count will rise to 12.6 million shares by the end of the year.

If I divide a market cap of $792 million by 12.6 million shares, I get an updated price target of $63. That’s 9% lower than Kura Sushi USA’s current price of $69 per share. While I think management’s full-year revenue guidance for 2026 may be too low, this stock still looks like it could be overvalued even if you consider that factor. And there’s still 15.97% short interest for Kura Sushi USA as well, which indicates that short sellers still think it’s overvalued.

Why Buying Based on Price Action Alone Is Risky

If I use the valuation methods that I use to analyze other restaurant stocks, Kura Sushi USA looks overvalued right now. So, while price action indicates that this company’s stock price could be rising toward another peak on the chart right now, its financial results would need to be better to justify the price at that peak. And while Kura Sushi USA did post better-than-expected 1Q 2026 results, it still reported a loss and negative comps for the quarter. Additionally, the restaurant sector is still under a lot of pressure because consumers are cutting back their spending. So, price action by itself isn’t enough to justify a buy rating for this stock.

Conclusion

If you look at Kura Sushi USA’s current EV/EBITDA, it’s trading at a valuation similar to other growth stocks in the restaurant sector. While I do think it could outperform analysts’ expectations in upcoming quarters and management may be providing conservative guidance, this stock isn’t exactly cheap, either. In fact, many SA analysts have called it a sell recently, and the SA Quant score says it’s a strong sell. So, even though the chart indicates that this stock could rise further, I maintain my hold rating.

data.title: Teekay Tankers: Built For A Cycle, Not Just A Spike
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

The tanker business is firing on all cylinders.

Despite a 1.9 million barrel drop in U.S. crude in the previous month, day rates remain close to five-year highs.

The company that I’m going to dissect in this piece directly works within this environment. Yes, it’s none other than Teekay Tankers (TNK).

Teekay operates and owns some of the largest oil tankers in the world, transporting crude along long international routes. Its fleet generates mainly through spot rates, thus an increase in demand and longer routes run directly into revenue and cash.

Arguably, oil is killing it right now and giants like Chevron Corporation (CVX) and Exxon Mobil (XOM) grab most of the attention. Nonetheless, companies like Teekay succeed where disruption is an instant profit generator.

Q3 is a strong example. Even with a lean fleet, a two-punch combo of intelligent vessel deployment and operational discipline gave steady revenues and reliable cash flow.

Timing and cost control are equally important. Teekay's balance sheet strength aids it in handling market fluctuations and still returning cash to shareholders.

Furthermore, TNK trades at a sensible level right now. It provides a buffer in case rates normalize and still harvests the upside if the cycle stays favorable.

Of course, there are risks but considering everything, I would consider Teekay a ‘Buy’. Its operational flexibility and disciplined execution place it in a good position across the cycle.

Oil Demand Is Doing the Heavy Lifting for Tankers

Interestingly, the oil supply (globally) is entering 2026 strongly. This is backed by the fact that since Q4 2024, OPEC and non-OPEC supply expanded around 1.6 million and 2.4 million barrels per day (bpd), respectively.

Meanwhile, the demand growth was only 900,000 bpd, which was lower than the historical value of 1.2-1.3 million. This can potentially result in an initial overcapacity of approximately 3 million bpd in H1 2026.

That said, the increasing tonne-mile demand is outpacing the total oil volumes due to increased shipping distances, sanctions, port restrictions and rerouted trade.

In the meantime, the trend is being supported by pricing dynamics. The consumption and restocking are being aided by lower oil prices and a weakened US dollar.

To top this, China is said to be stocking as much as 169 million barrels in its reserves, directly absorbing surplus supply and boosting seaborne flows.

To spot exposed carriers such as Teekay, these market conditions soothe visibility of rates and cash generation. They look more sustainable rather than just an abrupt spike.

Teekay Q3 2025 Earnings

I consider Q3 2025 to be an example that smart fleet moves can convert hot markets into actual profits.

Profitability Rises Despite a Leaner Fleet

GAAP net income of $92.1 million seems nice on the surface, but inside it carries $25.9 million from vessel sales which may hide the core performance. Adjusted net income is a better measure. It rose 9% to $53.3 million on a sequential basis.

This impressive growth happened despite Teekay selling 5 vessels and repurchasing one in-chartered vessel. It shows that profitability is a matter of positioning and performance rather than fleet size alone.

The market set up favored this, as Q3 Aframax and LR2 spot rates averaged $33,400 and $31,100, respectively. In addition, VLCCs were at an average of $30,900, way above the long-run Q3 averages.

The surge in early Q4 rates also saw Suezmax increase to over 45,500 a day, over 60% higher annually. This validates that Q3 strength was not an effect of seasonality but an indicator of tightness in the tanker market. The spot exposure and the flexibility of operations placed Teekay in a superb place to capitalize on this upside.

Net revenue fell by a mere 1.6% to $146.3 million, though there were fewer vessels and revenue days. The impact of sold and returned tankers was compensated for by the acquisition of three new ones in the recent quarters and a notable reduction in the downtime of dry-docks.

The timing and management of the fleet were also as significant as the fleet size, and the company was able to overcome the headwinds without damaging the top line.

Turning Rate Strength into Real Cash

Strong spot rates only make a difference when they translate into cash. Teekay has shown in Q3 that it can make that happen.

The cost of charter hire reduced by 17%, or $1.9 million, following the re-delivery of the in-chartered vessel. Additionally, general and administrative costs dropped 23% to $11,146 as past quarter’s equity compensation was swept off.

There was a negligible increase in vessel operating expenses, up by less than 1%. The costs were tamed and the increased rates were passed directly through to earnings. This signaled strong operating leverage.

That leverage was most visible in the generation of cash. With a dry dock and capital expenditure of $5.5 million, FCF was at $68.7 million.

Furthermore, Q3 spot rates were approximately 3 times higher than the breakeven of the fleet of roughly $11,300 per day.

The management has shown that any additional cost above breakeven will increase the profit by about $1.66 per share in a year. In a firm rate environment, cash generation is repeatable as well as scalable.

Last but not least, another point of strength is the balance sheet. Teekay had $775 million available in the form of cash and short-term investments. This allowed it the flexibility to decide when to replace the fleet, absorb market fluctuations and give capital back without stress.

Valuation Analysis

Over the last year, TNK stock has been following an uneven but clearly ascending trend.

TNK stock currently trades at around $58, which is remarkably higher than the recent consolidation ranges. At this level, TNK is sitting above all the moving averages (see the above snapshot).

This positioning matters. TNK is over 20% ahead of its 200-day moving average and it shows a meaningful long-term uptrend. Although the recent spike might cause a temporary cooling or sideways action, this appears like momentum returning and not a blow-off action.

Additionally, the wider technical configuration is supportive, provided that the stock is trading above the low-to-mid $50s.

Earnings and cash flow wise, the company is fairly valued as a cyclical tanker enterprise, which is still generating significant cash flow.

The analysts’ consensus estimates a normalized EPS of $6.59 in FY2025 that are projected to grow to $6.93 in 2026 and $7.13 in 2027.

Moreover, they estimate the stock to trade at 8.9x 2025 and 8.5x 2026 earnings. These multiples are higher but still do not presuppose peak-cycle conditions. The valuation is fair and it represents a strong operating environment.

What the Peer Comparison Really Says

Scorpio Tankers (STNG) is a direct peer with its product tanker focus, spot rate exposure, and a similar fleet profile.

STNG trades at approximately 10x forward non-GAAP earnings and 5x forward EV/EBITDA. Although such multiples still hold in absolute terms, they are higher than TNK's, which still trades at lower earnings and enterprise value multiples.

The distinction does not lie in the quality of business, but in expectations. STNG is valued at an increased, more sustained cycle, presupposing that the rates and utilization remain high. On the other hand, TNK is valued in a normal environment, although both are subject to the same market conditions.

The difference is evident at the asset level. STNG trades at approximately 0.86x book value, and Teekay now trades at 1x.

Nonetheless, trading near book does not imply that the stock is overpriced. Even in a capital-heavy cyclical industry, 1x book is a sign of caution. Teekay appears less cheap than it used to be, but is still fairly priced in the sector and against peers.

At a base case of $6.75 to $7 in normalized EPS and an 8x multiple, fair value comes around $55-$60, making the current price of $58 reasonably priced.

In a bullish case, given the spot rates remain solid and earnings reach $7.25-$7.75, a 9x multiple would increase the price to $63-$68.

All in all, TNK does not require an optimistic rate surprise to deliver. Its performance supports the generation of solid returns.

Risks To My Thesis

The immediate risk is a sudden decline in the spot rates. While Teekay has a low breakeven, its earnings are very sensitive to the day rates. If the Aframax and Suezmax rates slide substantially to mid-cycle levels and remain there, the cash generation would come under pressure.

I am comfortable with normalization, however, a steep or prolonged drop would decrease the earnings visibility and limit the upside, despite a healthy balance sheet.

Lastly, the large cash position of Teekay is a strength, yet it increases the risk of execution. Valuation could be eroded by overpaying for vessels at the end of the cycle, poor judgment of fleet renewal timing, or by making committed Capital returns when the rates are beginning to flatten.

These risks are unlikely to dominate the picture, but I would be monitoring them closely.

Takeaway

So, where does this leave us?

The tanker market is tight and structurally robust. Sanctions, extended voyages, and rerouted cargoes are continuing to keep rates high in VLCC, Suezmax and Aframax segments. Teekay is in the sweet spot. It transforms these conditions into real and repeatable cash using clever fleet management, operational flexibility, and a robust balance sheet. Even with a smaller fleet, Q3 demonstrated the ability of the company to preserve earnings and generate free cash flows. Valuation is decent, leaving room if the rates normalize but still enjoying the upside if the cycle plays out. Indeed, Teekay is in a position to deliver results across the entire cycle.

data.title: Lock In Smart Income With 7% Yields Before The Large-Cap Bubble Bursts
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Large-cap stocks have been utterly dominant in recent years. If you don't have overweight exposure to the largest stocks in the market, your portfolio has probably underperformed the S&P 500 index.

The S&P 500 is an index that is weighted by market cap. The larger the market cap, the more it buys.

The S&P 500 equal-weighted, dramatically underperformed the S&P 500 in 2025.

If we look at the historical picture, we can see that this is a relatively new development. As of January 2020, the S&P 500 equal-weighted had outperformed the S&P 500 since inception by a lot, with the exception of the Dot-com bubble:

For the past three years, we've seen the S&P 500 materially outperform the S&P 500 Equal Weighted – nearly doubling its performance:

What this means is that if you didn't hold the biggest companies, you underperformed. Even if you held the largest companies, but simply equal-weighted your portfolio, you underperformed.

Party Like It's 1999

Something similar happened from 1997 to 1999:

The problem with so much upside being tied to the largest companies is that the larger they are, the harder they fall. Investors think about the Dot-com crash today, and they talk about all the small Dot-coms that failed. Yet, the real brunt of the sell-off was in the largest companies in 2000:

There is a bit of an aura of invincibility surrounding large caps today. Will it fall apart in 2026? Not necessarily. But the outperformance of large caps is something that historically proved unsustainable.

I'm not guaranteeing that it will happen this year. What I am saying is that the huge gap between large-cap and small/mid-cap valuations should have investors thinking about what they are buying. Source

If your goal is to "buy low," then you should be focused on investments that are trading at lower valuations. We're going to look at two funds that invest in small-cap stocks and will provide you with exposure to investments that are trading at low valuations today.

Pick No. 1: SCAP – Yield 6.5%

Infrastructure Capital Small Cap Income ETF (SCAP) is an ETF that focuses on small-cap stocks seeking market exposure that is comparable to the Bloomberg 2000 U.S. Value Total Return Index.

SCAP uses many of the strategies we see with its sister funds in other sectors. It utilizes modest leverage, active management, and targeted options writing. As of Q3, SCAP was invested about 85% in common equities and 15% in preferred equities.

Its largest allocations are banks, mortgage REITs, and finance/insurance. But with less than 33% in those three categories combined, it is highly diversified: Source

SCAP is a relatively young ETF, but it is off to a strong start, outperforming the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD).

We should note that since SCAP does use leverage, we can expect it to be more volatile. While we expect higher returns in the long run, we expect larger swings in price.

SCAP pays a monthly dividend, which it increased twice in 2025 from $0.19/month to $0.205/month. At year's end, SCAP makes an oversized payment if it has any undistributed taxable income. In 2025, the final dividend was $0.24.

Not bad for a year when small caps underperformed large caps. We look forward to seeing what SCAP can do when small caps outperform.

Pick No. 2: RVT – Yield 7.1%

Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT) is a CEF (closed end fund) that has a very similar investment style. Like SCAP, RVT focuses on small-cap stocks using a value-oriented investment selection.

RVT has a much longer track record, outperforming the Russell 2000 since its inception in 1986. Source

In particular, if we zoom in to the early 2000s when the gap between large-cap and small-cap valuations was similar to today, RVT outperformed the S&P 500 significantly.

RVT is structured as a CEF; unlike ETFs, CEF shares are free to trade at premiums or discounts to NAV. RVT is trading at nearly a 10% discount to NAV, creating an attractive buying opportunity.

RVT pays a variable dividend, based on 7% of the trailing 4-quarter NAV. As a result, each quarter the dividend will change slightly, going up if NAV is higher than it was last year and going down if NAV is lower. Since it is based on the average over four quarters, the changes are typically small and occur gradually over time. Then in Q4, RVT pays out any excess taxable income in an oversized dividend.

In 2025, RVT paid $1.42 in dividends, which was 8.2% of its NAV at the start of the year. We expect the next payment in March to be $0.30/share.

Conclusion

We hold many small-cap stocks in our portfolio, but holdings like RVT and SCAP invest in numerous companies that will not be candidates for our investing strategy. Adding these funds to your portfolio provides more diversification in the small-cap space and makes the bet that the huge valuation gap between large-caps and small-caps will return to normal.

While other investors are buying large caps, hoping that the valuations can remain high indefinitely, we are focused on buying investments that are trading at low valuations and provide us with immediate cash flow today. We are steadily realizing our gains through dividends, where we can use the cash-flow to invest in the same companies, invest in different companies, or do something really crazy like take some cash out of our brokerage.

While others chase unrealized gains, we have realized gains flowing into our portfolios as cash every single month. That's the Income Method.

data.title: AstraZeneca: Oncology Dominance Justifies New All-Time Highs
data.description:
Since my last article, "AstraZeneca: Oncology Breakthroughs And Farxiga Surge Excite Investors," AstraZeneca shares (NASDAQ:AZN) (OTCPK:AZNCF) have risen 26.9%.

In the 1-D chart, the first thing I saw was the bears trying to push its stock below $90. But this correction lasted only 10 days, from December 8 to December 18.

After which, according to my assessment, Wave 5, the final leg of the impulsive move, resumed. Wave 5, in turn, will end upon reaching the third standard deviation band formed from the VWAP, namely $103.2.

If I put TA aside, does AstraZeneca still deserve a 'Buy' rating?

AstraZeneca's R&D is unique and has made it a leader in the oncology market

I want to say right here, unlike most Big Pharma players, AstraZeneca doesn't have blockbusters like AbbVie's Rinvoq/Skyrizi (ABBV) or Novo Nordisk's Ozempic (NVO).

It also doesn't make high-profile deals such as the one Pfizer (PFE) signed with Metsera on November 7.

Yes, AstraZeneca concluded relatively rare but highly successful deals with other leaders in the industry [Fusion Pharmaceuticals, CSPC, or Daiichi Sankyo], which I think were necessary to strengthen its position in the radiopharmaceuticals markets and immunological diseases markets.

But I believe that AstraZeneca under CEO Pascal Soriot is more active than its competitors, Merck (MRK), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and Takeda (TAK), in strengthening its position in the oncology and rare disease drugs markets.

By the way, IQVIA (IQV) expects the global spending on anti-cancer medications to grow to $409 billion by 2028.

And in AstraZeneca's oncology division, I think Imfinzi has the greatest influence on its EBIT in the short term.

Sales of this anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody were $1.6 billion in Q3, up 33.1% year-over-year, and beating even my 'best case' scenario by $75 million.

In my view, growth in demand for Imfinzi primarily reflects its label expansions.

On March 17th, it was approved, as monotherapy, by the EU for the treatment of limited-stage small cell lung cancer. Eleven days later, on March 28, Imfinzi, in combination with cisplatin and gemcitabine, received FDA approval to treat people with MIBC [muscle invasive bladder cancer].

By the way, I wanted to find the number of MIBC cases in WHO's Cancer Tomorrow.

To my disappointment, it doesn't provide figures only for this subtype, but even without that, it's clear that the TAM is large, as about 131,144 cases of bladder cancer [102.8 thousand men and 28.4 thousand women] will be diagnosed in U.S. alone in 2050, up 63.6% increase from 2022.

Meanwhile, during a Q3 call, David Fredrickson, EVP of AZN's oncology business division, sounded confident about Imfinzi.

This was another outstanding quarter for our I/O franchise with growth of Imfinzi and Imjudo of 31% and 14%, respectively.

We see continued enthusiasm for Imfinzi in the new lung indications, ADRIATIC and AEGEAN and in bladder cancer with NIAGARA, alongside further expansion in our more established indications such as HIMALAYA and CASPIAN.

We are also starting to see early signs of adoption of MATTERHORN in the U.S. following its Category 1 NCCN guideline inclusion and eagerly await regulatory decisions.

In Q4, AstraZeneca had several Imfinzi-related milestones that strengthened my faith in it.

So, on November 25, the FDA gave the green light to durvalumab in combination with FLOT chemotherapy for the treatment of patients with early gastric and gastroesophageal junction cancers.

Here I will pause to say that FLOT is a combination of 4 drugs: fluorouracil, leucovorin, oxaliplatin, and docetaxel.

The approval was based on the results of a Phase 3 MATTERHORN study [NCT04592913].

In this trial, the Imfinzi+FLOT group lowered the risk of death by 22% versus SoC chemotherapy [P=0.021, HR=0.78].

And, the estimated 24-month event-free survival rate was 67.4% for patients receiving durvalumab plus FLOT versus 58.5% for the FLOT-only arm.

But there are two more "gems" in AstraZeneca's oncology franchise that few people usually pay attention to.

Enhertu and Datroway made AstraZeneca the leader of the ADCs market

Here I must pause to note that Datroway and Enhertu were not discovered in-house at AstraZeneca.

Both drugs were developed by Daiichi Sankyo (DSKYF).

On March 28, 2019, AstraZeneca announced a deal worth up to $6.9 billion with Daiichi to develop Enhertu [trastuzumab deruxtecan], or DS-8201 as it was then known.

Meanwhile, a new strategic collaboration, this time for the development of Datroway [DS-1062], was announced on July 27, 2020.

This TROP2-directed ADC was first approved by the FDA 5 years after Enhertu.

But in my view, it is quickly becoming the 'preferred choice' for oncologists in the treatment of breast cancer and EGFR-mutated non-small cell lung cancer.

So, Datroway generated $24 million in revenue in Q3, up 118.2% YoY. AstraZeneca forecasts its peak sales will exceed $5 billion.

I believe AstraZeneca's goal is achievable, especially given the results of the TROPION-Breast02 study [NCT05374512], which were announced on October 19.

According to data, patients with metastatic TNBC treated with Datroway had a median overall survival of 23.7 months, which is 5 months longer than those in the chemotherapy group [P=0.0291].

In addition, it reduced the risk of disease progression/death by 43% [P<0.0001].

EVP Susan Galbraith was as excited as I was about datopotamab deruxtecan's prospects after these data.

In addition, almost 2/3 of patients experienced a complete or partial response to Datroway, double the rate seen with chemotherapy, alongside a manageable safety profile, low rates of discontinuation and no treatment-related deaths.

These data clearly differentiate Datroway and together with its convenient 3-weekly dosing, position it to reshape the TNBC landscape for the 70% of first-line patients who are not suitable for immune checkpoint inhibitors.

However, AstraZeneca and Daiichi Sankyo aren't stopping there and are studying the efficacy of Datroway both as monotherapy [1st-line triple negative breast cancer] and in combination with Merck's Keytruda [1L NSCLC], rilvegostomig [various subgroups of non-small cell lung cancer], and Imfinzi.

I'm finished with Datroway. Now it's time for Enhertu.

Enhertu is also an antibody-drug conjugate, but it targets HER2 cancer cells.

It is already approved for 6 indications.

The most recent was its approval by the U.S. regulatory agency on December 15 last year in combination with Roche Holding's Perjeta (RHHBY) as a 1L treatment for people with HER2-positive metastatic breast cancer.

The FDA's decision is based on promising data from the DESTINY-Breast09 study [NCT04784715], in which this combination lowered the risk of death or disease progression by 44% compared to THP [taxane, trastuzumab, and pertuzumab].

The confirmed ORR was 85.1% versus 78.6%, respectively. But more telling to me is the 58 complete responses with Enhertu in combo with Perjeta compared to 33 in those patients who received THP.

And if I talk about the numbers for the 3rd quarter, they were again higher than I factored into my DCF model.

Revenue of Enhertu was $714 million, up 40% from Q3 2024 and 7.2% from the previous quarter. I expected sales to be more modest, $680 million, due to competition from Gilead's Trodelvy (GILD) and Roche's Kadcyla.

Risks

And again, risks. I found basically nothing bad in the Q3 report, except that Soliris sales were $15 million lower than my "base case" scenario.

Another setback is that the combination of Imfinzi and ceralasertib failed in the LATIFY Phase III trial.

Takeaway

AstraZeneca remains my favorite among Big Pharma players.

And according to Seeking Alpha, it will announce its Q4 numbers on February 10th. The consensus analyst estimate for AZN's EPS is $1.09, and revenue is $15.42 billion. However, I believe Wall Street is again setting the bar too low, as it has in nine of the last 11 quarters.

Overall, as a reminder.

AstraZeneca's non-GAAP EPS reached $1.19 in Q3. It rose by 14% year-on-year and 9.2% QoQ, thanks to drugs which I mentioned earlier, and also Farxiga and Ultomiris.

These two medications are flagships in cardiovascular and rare disease franchises, which generated $2.13 billion [up 10.1% YoY] and $1.23 billion [18.8%] for AstraZeneca, respectively.

And given the strong results from the Phase III TULIP-SC study published on January 6th, in which Saphnelo demonstrated efficacy in the treatment of an autoimmune disorder called systemic lupus erythematosus, I continue to cover AstraZeneca with a 'Buy' rating.

data.title: January Dogs Of The Dow: One Ideal 'Safer' Dividend Buy
data.description:
Foreword

While most of this collection of Dow Industrials is too pricey and reveals only skinny dividends, January (again) finds only Verizon (VZ), living up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share price and it is also ‘safer’ because it has more-than-enough free-cash-flow to support its dividends.

In late February 2024 Dow Jones replaced the low priced high yield dividend payer Walgreens, with Amazon (AMZN), a high-priced non-dividend payer. More recently, in November 2024, Intel (INTC) was replaced by Nvidia (NVDA) and Dow Inc (DOW) was supplanted by Sherwin-Williams (SHW).

With renewed downside market pressure up to 72.52% all ten of the top ‘safer’ January Dow dividend dogs could become elite fair-priced buys with annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced Dow Dogs in Actionable Conclusion 21 near the mid-point of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 17.86% To 33.07% Net Gains From Top-Ten Dow Dogs By January 2027

Four of ten top dividend-yielding Dow dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this January, 2027 yield-based forecast for Dow dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 40% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1,000.00 invested in each of the ten highest-yielding Dow stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2026-27 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to January, 2027 were:

NVIDIA (NVDA) was projected to net $330.70, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 64 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 132% greater than the market as a whole.

Microsoft (MSFT) was projected to net $285.12, based on the median of target price estimates from 58 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 8% greater than the market as a whole.

Verizon (VZ) was projected to net $247.41, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 66% Under the market as a whole.

Procter & Gamble (PG) was projected to net $238.39 based on the median of target prices estimated by 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 61% under to the market as a whole.

Salesforce (CRM) was projected to net $237.05, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 58 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% over the market as a whole.

Nike (NKE) was projected to net $233.91, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 39 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 28% over the market as a whole.

Amazon (AMZN) was projected to net $213.30, based on the median of target price estimates from 67 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 37% more than the market as a whole.

Coca-Cola (KO) was projected to net $198.76, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 61% less than the market as a whole.

Honeywell (HON) was projected to net $191.74 based on the median of target price estimates from 26 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 5% under the market as a whole.

Disney (DIS) was projected to net $178.56, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 31 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 44% above the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 23.55% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten Dow Index stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 8% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

30 Dow Dogs By 1Yr Total Returns

30 Dow Dogs By Analyst Target Gain Estimates

The January, 2026 Dow 30 By Yield

No January, 2026 Dow 30 Dogs Made the Top Ten in All Three Ranks: Returns, Targets, & Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top January Dow Dividend Stocks By Yield Ranged 2.51% To 6.88%

Top ten Dow Dogs represented five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

The lone communication services dog placed first, Verizon [1], to lead the pack. Then, in second place was another loner; this one from the energy sector, Chevron [2].

Four healthcare stocks placed third, sixth, ninth, and tenth: Merck (MRK) [3], Amgen (AMGN) [6], United Health (UNH) [9], then Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) [10].

Two January consumer staple stalwarts, placed fourth and fifth, Procter& Gamble (PG) [4], and Coca Cola Co (KO) [5].

Finally, two consumer discretionary members placed seventh and eighth, Home Depot (HD) [7], and Nike (NKE) [8], to complete the January top-ten Dogs of the Dow by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The graphic above shows the relative strengths of the top ten January Dow dogs by dividend and price as of market close 1/7/2026.

Nearly all top-ten Dow dogs show a less than ideal status because the prices of nine exceed the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in each.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by just one of the five lowest priced Dogs of the Dow. Only Verizon, lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices.

Dow Index Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 1/7/26

The above chart reveals thirty Dow dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

Two stocks in the Dow index pay no dividend and are, therefore, excluded from the calculation. For those that count, December’s least expensive dog of the Dow is Verizon $14.54 per dividend dollar and the most expensive is NVIDIA (NVDA) at $4,716.98 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): One of Twenty-Eight Dow Dividend Dogs Show No or Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

No or a negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividend, The January pair are Coca-Cola, and 3M. They are considered unsafe to buy due to dividend yields equaling or exceeding free cash flow yields.

Two cut their dividends in the 2020 Ides of March depression times, Boeing, and The Walt Disney Co. One more Salesforce (CRM), snuck onto the Dow index without a dividend.

However, in March 2023, Salesforce declared its first-ever quarterly payout and Disney, in January, 2024 rejoined the dividend paying ranks. However four (two short on cash and two non-dividend payers, Amazon, and Boeing) cast a pall on the Dow by being stockholder adverse.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, TACO deals, wars, ICE, and vigilante actions, too.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases or Rerates Could Get All Ten Dow Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, one in the top ten is already ideally priced. Beside Verizon, already in the ideal zone, nine more top-yield stocks are within $253.15 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends or split shares. Those options may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 17.53% To 34.05% Top Ten Dow Index Upsides To January, 2027; (32), Three -2.41 to -6.00% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 34.12% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Dow Dogs To January, 2027

Ten-top Dow January updated yield (dividend / price) results, as verified by YCharts, made the following ranking:

Dow top ten noted 1/7/26 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors. (Real Estate is not reported by the Dow and Utilities has its own Dow Index. Materials, Financials, Industrials, and Technology also missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of the Ten Highest-Yield Dow Dogs (34) To Deliver 19.27% Vs. (35) 14.37% Net Gains by All Ten Come January, 2027

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dow Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 34.12% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The very lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Verizon, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 24.74%.

The five lowest-priced Dow top-yield dogs for January were: Verizon; Nike; Cola-Cola; Merck; Procter & Gamble, with prices ranging from $40.13 to $138.04.

Five higher-priced Dow top-yield dogs as of January 7 were: Chevron; Johnson & Johnson; Amgen; UnitedHealth; Home Depot, with prices ranging from $155.20 to $349.06.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the feckless Fed and Trade war-fueled slide gains momentum into 2026, with the exception of a very few Techno, and (maybe) Energy stocks, look for at least half of the ten Dow Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or quite suddenly.

Afterword

Buy One ‘Safer' Dow January DiviDog and Watch Four More

Early January 2026, 19 of the 30 Dow Index were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 1/7/26 data showed one was IDEAL and fair-priced.

How Dow 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price” Result For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that one dividend from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend Dow dogs conforms to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or less than) the price of a single share. That one is Verizon Communications still standing after passing the ‘safer’ dividend gauntlet.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called “fair price.”

A market correction of 84.4% is required to put all 10 “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or create stock splits to bring prices the dogcatcher ideal level. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Dogs of the Dow updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnote

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Dow dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations. Just remember Verizon In December 2025 and the five stand-by Dow dogs of the month: Cisco, Chevron, Procter & Gamble, Honeywell, and McDonald’s.

data.title: Countdown To March: Jobs Report Locks In Rate Cut
data.description:
Friday’s jobs report for December was a double-edged sword in that it was extremely market friendly but not indicative of a strengthening economy. Investors liked it because the lack of job growth should move the Fed to cut rates at its March meeting by another 25 basis points, which further eases financial conditions and supports risk asset prices. Economists didn’t like it because it indicates that businesses are still leery of the economic outlook, due to tariffs, trade policy, and concerns about consumer resilience.

According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, the economy added 50,000 jobs last month, led by bars and restaurants, health care, and social assistance companies. Unfortunately, the prior two months were revised lower by 76,000, resulting in a net loss of 26,000 jobs. It is no coincidence that job growth fell off a cliff after April, following the rollout of President Trump’s trade policy. Tariffs are paid by businesses, which crimp profit margins and force owners to cut costs and limit expansion plans. The largest input cost for a business is labor. Perhaps tariffs will lead to an increase in business activity in the manufacturing sector that creates new jobs, but that hasn’t happened yet. We are paying the price upfront today for a payoff that some expect to come tomorrow. The ISM Manufacturing Index indicates that we have yet to see benefits, as it has been in contraction for ten consecutive months.

The loss of 268,000 federal workers clearly skewed the above results, but when we focus on private sector payrolls, the numbers are not that different. The private sector added 37,000 jobs last month, while the 70,000 in downward revisions to the prior two months left us with a loss of 33,000 jobs. This caps off the worst non-recession year for job creation since 2003. Yet we are likely to see a new normal, due to immigration policy, that requires fewer jobs each month to absorb the increase in labor supply. That number used to be approximately 100,000 per month, but it is now probably closer to 50,000.

On the wage front, average hourly earnings increased 0.3% in December and 3.8% over the past year. While the rate of growth has declined, which has been a goal of the Fed’s to bring down the rate of inflation, it is still holding above the rate of inflation. Real income growth is critical to continue realizing real consumer spending growth.

The unemployment rate edged lower from 4.5% to 4.4%, but that was largely due to a decline in the participation rate from 62.5% to 62.4%. Participation is likely to continue declining, given immigration policy, which will keep a lid on the unemployment rate. A lower unemployment rate looks better, but the primary driver of our labor force growth has been immigration. Labor force growth is an important source of economic growth. Slower labor force growth will lead to a slower rate of economic growth over time.

Therefore, the no-hire and no-fire labor market is still with us. If I have one concern, it is that the downward revisions in the number of jobs created are being accompanied by a decline in the length of the workweek. Usually, when job growth slows, we see an increase in the length of the workweek. Employers have fewer workers who work longer hours to make up the difference, but that is not the case today. The labor market is probably weaker than the headline numbers suggest, and that is why I think the Fed will act much sooner than the market expects with another rate cut. Following the jobs report, the probability of a rate cut at the March meeting was just 27%. I think over the coming weeks that will increase to a near guarantee, as the effective tariff rate is lowered by the Supreme Court, disinflation resumes, and the labor market remains soft.

The good news is that our frozen labor market has not adversely affected the rate of consumer spending growth, which is the primary engine of our economy. I think we can thank a raging bull market for that, as the wealth effect is powering spending for the top 50% of households, with a particular emphasis on the top 10%. This K-shaped economic expansion is not a problem for the market today, but when we approach that latest stage of this business cycle, it will be a key factor in how deep the inevitable drawdown is in the next bear market. I don’t see this as a factor in 2026.
